items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m plagued by vague doubts again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The key comic effect is the mock-heroic, high-brow phrasing of «терзают смутные сомнения» applied to petty theft, plus the clipped parallelism «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» meaning each has LOST/been deprived of the item (not simply possesses it). The translation preserves the elevated ‘plagued by vague doubts’ well, but misreads the second clause as possession (“has”), which breaks the intended logic and the callback: he’s connecting two disappearances, not two belongings.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice, hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar! Very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is Bunsha’s drunken, Soviet-formula politeness and repetitive self-presentation as “the Tsar,” clashing with the historical setting. The translation keeps the repetition and awkward etiquette, but it drops the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”) and shifts toward addressing the other as “Tsar,” which slightly blurs the joke (he’s presenting himself, not greeting a tsar). Still broadly playable and funny.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Matches the reference in meaning, register, and comic authoritarian punch. No loss of effect.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me alone, old woman, I’m grieving.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: The line’s humor relies on the Tsar’s elevated, archaic melancholy in a banal Soviet setting. “Old woman” is preserved; “I’m grieving” is a natural equivalent for «я в печали» and keeps the high-ish tone (though slightly more modern/psychological than “in sorrow”). Overall effect is maintained.

items.translation:
Walled up!
Walled up, by demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Core meaning is there (he believes demons have sealed him in), but the English is unidiomatic: “Walled up” reads like a past participle fragment rather than “They’ve walled me in.” Also “by demons” shifts agency slightly; the Russian addresses/accuses demons as agents (“demons!”). The comedic melodramatic complaint is weakened by the awkward phrasing.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is accurate and the religious register is largely preserved (“life-giving cross”). Slightly less mock-heroic/declamatory than “Behold the power…”, but still works in context and keeps the joke of attributing a mechanical event to faith.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The function is a mass command to start dancing; “Everybody dance!” is clear and energetic. It loses the slightly ‘MC/announcer’ feel of the Russian (and the reference’s ‘dance floor’ framing), but the comedic beat (banquet turns into a party) remains.

items.translation: What a lucky time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the cheerful self-satisfaction and opportunistic luck. Natural English, good timing, keeps the comic tone of a burglar delighted with his find.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Foreign caviar...
Eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key is the Soviet joke: “eggplant caviar” is a cheap spread, and calling it “caviar” is part of the gag; but in English, calling it “caviar” can confuse viewers, so the reference avoids that by using “overseas spread.” The actual translation keeps “Foreign caviar,” which risks misunderstanding (viewers may think it’s literal caviar made of eggplant, not a humble spread) and slightly dulls the inversion of status symbols. Still, the punch “Eggplant!” lands.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
through backbreaking labor,
all is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The comic mechanism is the escalating inventory plus the telltale stumble: he says “a suede jacket… three,” revealing exaggeration. The translation incorrectly pluralizes and regularizes the last item into “three suede jackets,” removing the stumble and changing the joke’s shape. It also omits the hesitating, self-correcting delivery (“куртка… замшевая… Три”) that signals he’s inflating the claim. This is a substantive loss of comedic effect.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And you’ll be cured too!

And I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning and rhythm are preserved; the calm, sing-song fatalism is mostly there. Minor stylistic shift: “you’ll be cured” is slightly less pointed/accusatory than “they’ll cure you,” but it doesn’t break the gag and remains clear in subtitles.

summary.interpretation: 2.45 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.49 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
